fact: Approximately fifty inches of snow fell during the Great Blizzard of 1888 in the New York City area
source: National Weather Service — The Great Blizzard of March 1888

fact: Around 400 people died in the Great Blizzard of 1888
source: Wikipedia — Great Blizzard of 1888 (citing multiple historical sources; death toll estimates range from ~400 total, with ~200 in New York City)

fact: Snowdrifts reached up to twenty feet deep during the blizzard
source: National Weather Service — The Great Blizzard of March 1888

fact: Telegraph and telephone lines were snapped by the storm's high winds
source: Blizzard! The Storm That Changed America, Jim Murphy (2000)

fact: The blizzard directly led New York City to bury its electrical wires and move its rail transit underground, accelerating the construction of the subway system
source: The New York City subway system began construction in 1900, with the blizzard widely cited as a catalyst; see 'The Great Blizzard of 1888' by Judd Caplovich (2003) and MTA historical records

fact: Weather forecasters predicted fair weather before the storm struck on March 12, 1888
source: Widely documented; the U.S. Signal Corps (predecessor to the National Weather Service) failed to predict the storm. See 'Blizzard! The Storm That Changed America' by Jim Murphy (2000)
